description: The flower of a plant, especially one producing fruit; also the act of flowering. 'Phirali' names the moment of emergence—when buds break into color, when potential becomes visible beauty, the flowering that precedes fruiting.
sound_symbolism: The word opens into bloom. 'Phi' /ɸi/ begins with soft breath, the bud stirring. Then 'ra' reaches outward as petals extend, and 'li' lifts into the light, completing the movement from enclosed potential to visible living form.
grammatical_notes: Noun: 'lo phirali' (blossoms). Under the nature-now rule, a counted blossom may take optional 'lipha' while attached to a living plant and optional 'themo' once detached and treated as an object. The flowering itself is a verb of its own: 'peloru sheloa.' (The flower blooms.) Related to 'peloru' (flower), but phirali names the moment of flowering as a thing beheld.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Blossoming teaches that beauty emerges in its own time—forcing hastens nothing, while patient conditions allow flowering to occur naturally.
pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Blossoms were the supreme motif of Art Nouveau—the moment of opening, the curve of petals, the ephemeral beauty that must be honored before it passes.
